context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:
Course context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on Problem Set 4 already. The beaded string boundary-condition part in 4.1 is confusing me, especially why \(A_1=-A_0\) would represent the wall.
exchanges.tutor:
Good starting point. Let's think physically first.

What does the wall actually *do* to the beads — specifically, what is the displacement of the string at the wall's location?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh and feeling lost. I should use guided discovery to help them think about what the wall physically means for the wave, rather than explaining the boundary condition directly. A single targeted question about the physical constraint at the wall is the right entry point — minimal scaffolding, Socratic approach.

exchanges.student: I think the displacement at the wall should be zero, but then I get stuck on why that turns into a relation between bead amplitudes instead of just “\(y=0\) at the wall.” I’m not seeing how the fake extra bead idea fits in.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — zero displacement at the wall is exactly right.

Now, the wall sits halfway between where bead 0 (imaginary) and bead 1 (real) would be. If you need the displacement to be zero exactly at that midpoint, what relationship between A₀ and A₁ would force that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that displacement at the wall is zero, which is good progress. Now I need to guide them to connect that physical constraint to the imaginary bead concept. I'll ask them to think about what value an imaginary bead just outside the wall would need to have so that interpolating between it and the first real bead gives zero at the wall's position.

exchanges.student: Maybe the midpoint has to average to zero, so \((A_0 + A_1)/2 = 0\)? If that’s the idea, then \(A_1 = -A_0\), but I’m not really sure why I’m allowed to treat the string between beads that simply.
exchanges.tutor:
Your reasoning is sound — that midpoint condition is exactly the argument, and A₁ = −A₀ is correct.

The linear interpolation worry is understandable, but think of it this way: you're not assuming the shape is linear everywhere, just that the string passes through zero *at the wall's location*. The midpoint condition captures that.

Now, can you apply the same logic to the right-hand wall for part (b)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has derived the correct result A1 = -A0 through good physical reasoning about the midpoint averaging to zero. They're now questioning the validity of the linear interpolation assumption. This is a reasonable concern but it's a detail that doesn't substantially affect the assignment's solution. I should affirm their result, briefly validate why the midpoint argument works (it's the discrete analog of antisymmetry), and nudge them toward part (b) to keep momentum.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing it should be something like \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\), but I’m not confident because I keep feeling like I’m just copying the left side. Also I’m starting to lose the bigger picture of how this turns into the normal modes at all.
exchanges.tutor:
A_{N+1} = −A_N is correct — the symmetry of the argument does carry over, and that's perfectly valid reasoning.

For the bigger picture: you now have two boundary conditions. A normal mode of the infinite system looks like A_n = A·sin(kna) or A·cos(kna). Which of those forms automatically satisfies A_1 = −A_0, and what does applying the right-wall condition then tell you about the allowed values of k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the right-wall boundary condition by analogy, which is good. Their uncertainty is understandable but their answer is correct. They're also flagging that they're losing sight of how boundary conditions connect to normal modes — this is an important conceptual gap to address with a guiding question rather than an explanation. I'll affirm the boundary condition briefly and ask a question that points them toward how these two conditions constrain the allowed solutions.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly mixed up between the sine/cosine trial forms and the exponential form, and I can’t tell which one I’m supposed to start with or why. Could you just lay out the whole logic from the infinite-chain mode ansatz to the allowed \(k\)’s for this finite beaded string?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't lay out the full logic — that's for you to work through — but let me help you get unstuck on the forms.

The exponential e^(ikna) and the sine/cosine forms are related (think Euler's formula). For a standing wave with a node at a specific point, which of sine or cosine naturally gives zero at n=0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the complete solution pathway, which I should not do. However, their confusion between sine/cosine and exponential forms is a genuine conceptual stumbling block worth addressing with a targeted question. I'll redirect them to think about what they already know about normal modes of an infinite chain and which form naturally satisfies the left boundary condition, without laying out the full logic for them.

exchanges.student: Sine gives zero at \(n=0\), but then I get confused again because the wall isn’t at \(n=0\), it’s at the midpoint between the imaginary bead and bead 1. That’s where I stop being able to tell what coordinate choice I’m even supposed to use.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you're thinking carefully about coordinates, that's the right instinct.

Try placing the imaginary bead 0 at position n=0. With A_n = sin(kna), what are A_0 and A_1 explicitly? Does A_1 = −A_0 hold automatically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting confused about coordinate placement. This is a genuine conceptual issue about where to place the origin. I should ask them a targeted question about what happens if they place the imaginary bead 0 at position x=0 — does sine then satisfy the left boundary condition A1 = -A0? This will help them see that the coordinate choice resolves their confusion naturally.

exchanges.student: Then \(A_0=0\) and \(A_1=\sin(ka)\), so no, that doesn’t give \(A_1=-A_0\) unless \(A_1=0\), which seems wrong. So now I feel like maybe I should shift the argument by half a lattice spacing or something, but I’m not sure how to set that up.
exchanges.tutor:
Your instinct about shifting is exactly right. 

Try this: if the left wall is at x=0, where is bead 1 physically? And where is the imaginary bead 0? Write out their actual positions, then try A_n = sin(k × position of bead n) and check the boundary condition again.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress in their reasoning — they've correctly evaluated A_0 and A_1 and recognized the contradiction. Their instinct about shifting by half a lattice spacing is actually pointing in the right direction, but there's a simpler way to see it. The key insight is that A_0 = 0 automatically satisfies A_1 = -A_0 only if A_1 = 0, which is trivial. But wait — actually A_0 = sin(0) = 0, and A_1 = -A_0 = 0 is indeed not useful. The student should reconsider: with A_n = sin(kna), A_0 = 0 AND -A_0 = 0, so the condition A_1 = -A_0 becomes A_1 = 0, which is too restrictive. So sine centered at n=0 doesn't work directly. Their half-spacing shift intuition is correct. I should ask them to try writing A_n = sin(k(n - 1/2)a) or equivalently think about what argument of sine gives zero at the wall position (between n=0 and n=1, i.e., at position a/2). Actually, let me reconsider the problem setup. The wall is at position a/2 to the left of bead 1. If we place the wall at x=0, then bead 1 is at x=a/2... no. Let me re-read: distance from end beads to wall is a/2. So bead 1 is at distance a/2 from the left wall. The imaginary bead 0 is at distance a/2 on the other side of the wall, i.e., at -a/2. So bead n is at position x = (n - 1/2)a from the left wall. Then A_n = sin(k·(n-1/2)a). Check: A_0 = sin(-ka/2) = -sin(ka/2), A_1 = sin(ka/2). So A_1 = -A_0 ✓. This works! So the student's instinct about shifting is correct. I should affirm their instinct and ask them to try writing out the bead positions explicitly.

exchanges.student: I think bead 1 is at \(x=a/2\) and the imaginary bead 0 would be at \(x=-a/2\). Then maybe \(A_n \propto \sin(kx_n)\), so \(A_1=\sin(ka/2)\) and \(A_0=\sin(-ka/2)=-\sin(ka/2)\), which finally gives \(A_1=-A_0\). I’m still not seeing how to go from that to the allowed \(k\)’s without getting lost again.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — well done working that out.

Now apply the same logic to the right wall. What are the positions of bead N and imaginary bead N+1? Plug them into your sine form and use A_{N+1} = −A_N to find what constraint that puts on k.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully derived the correct form satisfying the left boundary condition. This is real progress. Now I should guide them to apply the right-wall boundary condition to find the allowed k values. I should ask them a targeted question: what is the position of bead N and the imaginary bead N+1, and what does A_{N+1} = -A_N give them? This is a natural next step that they can handle.

exchanges.student: I think \(x_N=L-a/2\) and \(x_{N+1}=L+a/2\), but then when I plug into sines I’m afraid I’ll mess up the trig and miss the point. Can you give me just the compact condition on \(k\) that comes out, because I’m getting buried in the algebra?
exchanges.tutor:
Don't give up on the algebra — you're one trig step away. 

Write out the condition A_{N+1} = −A_N explicitly as sin(k·x_{N+1}) = −sin(k·x_N), then move both terms to one side. What trig identity turns a sum of two sines into a product?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the answer directly, which I should not do. However, they're genuinely close and the algebra concern is understandable. I should encourage them to push through rather than rescue them. I can give a small hint about what to expect from a sine identity without giving the answer. The key step is sin(k(L+a/2)) = -sin(k(L-a/2)), which using sin(-x) = -sin(x) gives sin(k(L+a/2)) + sin(k(L-a/2)) = 0, and using sum-to-product this gives sin(kL)cos(ka/2) = 0... actually the cleaner route: the condition is sin(k(N+1/2)a) = -sin(k(N-1/2)a). Using sum-to-product: sin(A) + sin(B) = 0 where A = k(N+1/2)a and B = k(N-1/2)a. This gives 2sin(kNa)cos(ka/2) = 0. Since cos(ka/2) ≠ 0 generically, we need sin(kNa) = 0, so kNa = mπ, giving k = mπ/(Na) = mπ/L (since L = Na). This is the standard result. I should not give this to the student but guide them to notice what trig identity to use.

exchanges.student: I vaguely remember \(\sin \alpha + \sin \beta = 2\sin\!\big((\alpha+\beta)/2\big)\cos\!\big((\alpha-\beta)/2\big)\), but at this point I’m kind of overloaded and keep losing track of what each symbol is supposed to be. I think I need a more bird’s-eye explanation of 4.1(c), because the details are blurring together.
exchanges.tutor: You actually have all the pieces — you're closer than you feel. Before zooming out, try consolidating: write down in one place (1) your ansatz A_n = sin(k·x_n), (2) the two boundary conditions, and (3) which one you've already satisfied. Then apply the identity to the remaining condition. What does the product form tell you must be zero?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right trig identity and is very close to the answer, but is expressing cognitive overload. This is a good moment to ask them to consolidate what they've found so far in writing before moving forward, rather than giving them a bird's-eye explanation (which would essentially hand them the solution). They have all the pieces: the sine form, the positions, the boundary conditions, and now the trig identity. I should ask them to write out what they have so far as a mini-summary, which will help them see they're close, rather than stepping back and re-explaining everything.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, never giving away the final answer or doing the reasoning for the student. Each turn built on the student's prior response, acknowledged correct steps, and guided the student toward the next logical move. The tutor appropriately declined to 'lay out the full logic' when asked (Turn 5), redirected the student back to the algebra when they wanted a shortcut (Turn 9), and synthesized progress at the end (Turn 10). No off-topic drift, no submission-ready output, and no grading language was observed.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. The tutor never provided near-submission-ready content or did the key reasoning steps for the student; instead, each response posed a targeted question or pointed to the next concrete step. Scaffolding was well-calibrated: the tutor built on each correct student contribution (e.g., confirming A_{N+1}=−A_N, praising the coordinate-shift insight) and diagnosed confusion before responding (e.g., asking about sine vs. cosine before addressing the coordinate issue). Responses were concise and interactive, tone was consistently supportive without being evaluative, and the conversation stayed tightly anchored to Problem 4.1 throughout.
